sections.title: The German Genitive Case (Genitiv)
sections.description: The Genitive Case is primarily used to indicate possession, ownership, or a specific relationship between two nouns. While it is often replaced by the dative case in casual conversation (using von), the Genitive remains the standard for formal writing, literature, and precise academic language.

sections.title: 1. Core Function: Showing Possession
sections.description: The Genitive answers the question "Wessen?" (Whose?). It links two nouns together where the second noun "possesses" the first.

content.key_rule: Masculine and Neuter nouns usually add -es if the word is one syllable (e.g., des Mannes) and just -s if it has multiple syllables (e.g., des Computers).

translations.label: Masculine
translations.translation: The car of the father (The father's car).

sections.title: 2. Usage with Prepositions
sections.description: Certain prepositions "trigger" the Genitive case. When you use these words, the noun following them must be in the Genitive form.

triggers.title: Common Genitive Prepositions
triggers.description: These prepositions always require the Genitive case:

examples.german: Trotz des Regens gehen wir spazieren.
examples.translation: Despite the rain, we are going for a walk.

examples.german: Wegen des Wetters bleiben wir zu Hause.
examples.translation: Because of the weather, we stay at home.

sections.title: 3. Usage with Verbs and Adjectives
sections.description: Though less common in modern daily speech, several high-level verbs and adjectives require the Genitive.

triggers.title: Verbs
triggers.description: These verbs describe legal actions or internal reflections.

subcategories.label: Anklagen (To accuse of)
subcategories.german: Man klagte ihn des Mordes an.

subcategories.label: Sich schämen (To be ashamed of)
subcategories.translation: He is ashamed of his mistake.

triggers.title: Adjectives
triggers.description: These adjectives require the Genitive case:

subcategories.label: Würdig (Worthy of)
subcategories.translation: That is worthy of a king.

subcategories.label: Sicher (Sure of)
subcategories.german: Ich bin mir meiner Sache sicher.
subcategories.translation: I am sure of my case/point.

sections.title: 4. Possessive Pronouns in Genitive
sections.description: When using possessive pronouns (my, your, his) as independent modifiers in the Genitive, they follow the same endings as the indefinite articles.

example.note: Another example: Inmitten unserer Freunde. (In the midst of our friends.)

sections.title: 5. Adjective Declension in Genitive
sections.description: If an adjective sits between an article and a noun in the Genitive case, it almost always ends in -en.

translations.label: With Article
translations.translation: The house of the old man.

translations.label: Without Article (fresh coffee)
translations.translation: The scent of fresh coffee. (The adjective takes the ending the article would have had.)

content.key_rule: With an article, the adjective always ends in -en. Without an article (e.g., plural nouns or abstract concepts), the adjective takes the ending the article would have had.
